coredata.dc:title: Controlling the emission colour and chemical structure of carbon dots by catalysis-tuned conversion of ortho-aminophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Junkai
Ye, Kaiqi
Opoku, Henry
Li, Zhiqiang
Edman, Ludvig
Wang, Jia
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with tailored properties commonly requires time-consuming trial-and-error experimentation, in part because of a poorly understood and controlled chemical conversion of the precursor material. Here, we first report on the solid-state pyrolysis or solvothermal conversion of an ortho-aminophenol (oAP) precursor, comprising ortho-disposed amino and hydroxyl groups on a benzene ring. We find that both conversion reactions resulted in a two emission-colour product, which could be separated into distinct blue-emitting CDs (bCDs, λ peak = 420 nm) and yellow-emitting CDs (yCDs, λ peak = 565 nm) by repetitive column chromatography. Systematic characterization revealed that both CDs comprise a planar graphene-like interior, but that they are distinguished by that the bCDs comprise an intermixed significant amino-rich fluorophore while the yCDs instead comprise a pyridinic-rich fluorophore. This implies that the bCDs are formed via activation of the amino group of the oAP precursor, whereas the synthesis of the yCDs constituted a simultaneous activation of both the amino and hydroxyl groups. With this knowledge at hand, we managed to direct the chemical conversion of the oAP precursor to yield either solely bCDs or yCDs by adding a catalyst (either the Lewis acid AlCl3·6H2O or the Lewis base NaOH) that selectively and efficiently activated only one of the reaction pathways. This demonstration is important in that it shows that the synthesis of CDs with desired properties can be realized with efficient rational instead of trial-and-error means.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ortho-aminophenol precursor
Catalysis
Controlled reactivity
Tuned properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Controlling the conversion reaction for a desired emission colour

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Bertil & Britt Svenssons stiftelse för belysningsteknik

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Wallenberg Initiative Materials Science for Sustainability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Dr. Cheng Choo Lee for valuable advice during the TEM measurements, and the Ume\u00E5 Core Facility for Electron Microscopy (UCEM), the Vibrational Spectroscopy Core Facility (ViSp), and the XPS platform at Ume\u00E5 University for technical support. The authors also thank Dr. Yincai Xu at Link\u00F6ping University for valuable input regarding reaction mechanisms, and Prof. Christian Reichardt at Philipps University Marburg for appreciated input on solvatochromism. The authors finally acknowledge generous support from J. C. Kempes Minnes Stipendiefond (SMK-21-0015 and SMK-1956), the Swedish Research Council (2019-02345, 2020-04437 and 2021-04778), Bertil & Britt Svenssons stiftelse f\u00F6r belysningsteknik (2021 h\u00F6st-14 and 2022 h\u00F6st-31), the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation for a Proof of Concept grant (KAW 2022.0381), the Wallenberg Initiative Materials Science for Sustainability (WISE) funded by the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation (WISE-AP01-D02), and the European Union through an ERC Advanced Grant (ERC, InnovaLEC, 101096650).

The authors acknowledge Dr. Cheng Choo Lee for valuable advice during the TEM measurements, and the Ume\u00E5 Core Facility for Electron Microscopy (UCEM), the Vibrational Spectroscopy Core Facility (ViSp), and the XPS platform at Ume\u00E5 University for technical support. The authors also thank Dr. Yincai Xu at Link\u00F6ping University for valuable input regarding reaction mechanisms, and Prof. Christian Reichardt at Philipps University Marburg for appreciated input on solvatochromism. The authors finally acknowledge generous support from J. C. Kempes Minnes Stipendiefond (SMK-21-0015 and SMK-1956), the Swedish Research Council (2019\u201302345, 2020\u201304437 and 2021\u201304778), Bertil & Britt Svenssons stiftelse f\u00F6r belysningsteknik (2021 h\u00F6st-14 and 2022 h\u00F6st-31), the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation for a Proof of Concept grant (KAW 2022.0381), the Wallenberg Initiative Materials Science for Sustainability (WISE) funded by the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation (WISE-AP01-D02), and the European Union through an ERC Advanced Grant (ERC, InnovaLEC, 101096650).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A schematic of the chemical conversion of the oAP precursor to CDs by solid-state pyrolysis (top) or solvothermal reaction (bottom). (b) The PL intensity of the pyrolysis-converted “crude CDs” as a function of the excitation (λexc) and emission (λPL) wavelengths. (c) Photographs of the purified bCDs (left) and the purified yCDs (right) in ethanol solution (5 mg L−1) under daylight illumination and UV excitation (λpeak = 365 nm). (d) The normalized absorption spectrum of the oAP precursor (dashed line), the bCDs (open blue squares) and the yCDs (solid yellow circles). (e,f) The normalized PL spectrum of (e) the bCDs and (f) the yCDs measured at different excitation wavelengths, as identified in the upper right insets. The corresponding PLQY is displayed in the same insets. The PL and absorption spectra were measured in dilute ethanol solution with the solute concentration being 1 mg L−1. (g,h). The normalized PL transients of ethanol solutions of (g) bCDs and (h) yCDs (solute concentration = 1 mg L−1). The symbols represent the measurement data, while the solid black lines represent the fitting result. The fitting was executed with a single-exponential function and a constant background, and the derived values for the time constant τ and the corresponding value for chi-square χ2 are presented in the insets. The measurement was performed at room temperature, using a pulsed laser (λexc = 375 nm) as the excitation source. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The chemical structure of the oAP precursor. (b,c) The stepwise C–N coupling conversion of the oAP precursors into Dimer 1, Trimer 1, and a longer amino-rich moiety, that is incorporated into the bCD. (d,e) The stepwise combined CN and C–O coupling conversion of oAP into Dimer2, Trimer 2, and the pyridinic-rich moiety that is incorporated into the yCD.

(a,b) HRTEM images of discrete (a) bCDs and (b) yCDs deposited on a carbon film on a copper grid. The dotted white circles indicate the existence of CDs. Upper-right inset: enlargement of the ordered lattice structure (interplanar spacing of ∼0.22 nm) of a single CD; Lower-right inset: the measured diameter histogram, with the dashed line showing the fit with a log-normal function. (c, d) XRD pattern of a 100 μm thick film of (c) bCDs and (d) yCDs on a glass substrate. The main XRD peak is identified by the dashed line. (e,f) XPS survey spectra of 100 μm thick films of (e) bCDs and (f) yCDs on a silicon wafer. The derived atomic concentrations are indicated above the respective trace. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a–f) High-resolution XPS spectra in the (a, b) C 1s, (c, d) N 1s, and (e, f) O 1s regime of a 100 μm thick film of (a, c, e) bCDs and (b, d, f) yCDs on a silicon wafer. The measured data are presented by the open black circles, and the deconvoluted fitting are presented by the coloured lines and shadows. The solid black lines present the sum of the fitting. The assignment and atomic concentration are presented next to each fitted spectral component. The inset in (c) presents the chemical structure of the amino nitrogen (left) and the pyridinic nitrogen (right), while the “O∗-CO” in (e, f) refers to an oxygen atom connected by a single bond to a carbon atom in a carboxylic bond. (g–i) The normalized FTIR spectra of (g) the oAP precursor, (h) the bCDs and (i) the yCDs dispersed in a KBr powder. Key vibrational bands are identified by the dashed grey rectangles. (j) The chemical structure of the oAP precursor (top), the amino-rich moiety (middle) and the pyridinic-rich moiety (bottom). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The proposed reaction for the selective conversion of the oAP precursor to bCDs with the aid of an AlCl3·6H2O catalyst. (b) The selective pyrolysis conversion of the oAP precursor into bCDs with the aid of the AlCl3·6H2O catalyst. (c) The PL intensity of the catalyst-derived crude bCDs as a function of the excitation (λexc) and emission (λPL) wavelengths. The bCDs were dissolved in ethanol at a concentration of 1 mg L−1. (d) The selective conversion of the oAP precursor to yCDs with the aid of a NaOH catalyst. (e) The selective solvothermal conversion of the oAP precursor to yCDs with the aid of the NaOH catalyst. (f) The PL intensity of the catalyst-derived crude yCDs as a function of the excitation (λexc) and emission (λPL) wavelengths. The yCDs were dissolved in ethanol in a concentration of 1 mg L−1.

head.ce:title: Controlling the emission colour and chemical structure of carbon dots by catalysis-tuned conversion of ortho-aminophenol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Organic Photonics and Electronics Group

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) with tailored properties commonly requires time-consuming trial-and-error experimentation, in part because of a poorly understood and controlled chemical conversion of the precursor material. Here, we first report on the solid-state pyrolysis or solvothermal conversion of an ortho-aminophenol (oAP) precursor, comprising ortho-disposed amino and hydroxyl groups on a benzene ring. We find that both conversion reactions resulted in a two emission-colour product, which could be separated into distinct blue-emitting CDs (bCDs, λ peak = 420 nm) and yellow-emitting CDs (yCDs, λ peak = 565 nm) by repetitive column chromatography. Systematic characterization revealed that both CDs comprise a planar graphene-like interior, but that they are distinguished by that the bCDs comprise an intermixed significant amino-rich fluorophore while the yCDs instead comprise a pyridinic-rich fluorophore. This implies that the bCDs are formed via activation of the amino group of the oAP precursor, whereas the synthesis of the yCDs constituted a simultaneous activation of both the amino and hydroxyl groups. With this knowledge at hand, we managed to direct the chemical conversion of the oAP precursor to yield either solely bCDs or yCDs by adding a catalyst (either the Lewis acid AlCl3·6H2O or the Lewis base NaOH) that selectively and efficiently activated only one of the reaction pathways. This demonstration is important in that it shows that the synthesis of CDs with desired properties can be realized with efficient rational instead of trial-and-error means.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which are synthesized by a chemical conversion of benign precursor materials, can deliver a wide variety of emission colours at a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [1–3]. This positions CDs as a practical and environmentally preferrable alternative to more established luminescent compounds and materials for applications in, e.g., optical sensing [4], illumination [5], and biomedical applications [6]. However, the chemical conversion of the precursor material into CDs in the reactor is commonly poorly understood, in part because it can comprise several concurrent and interacting reactions [7]; this lack of understanding and control of the constituent CD formation process is hindering the rational and efficient development of CDs with tuned properties, notably a desired emission colour.

A common “brute force” method for obtaining a specific CD with desired properties is to employ several precursor materials during the conversion reaction, and then perform an extensive purification process that isolates the specific CD that exhibits the desired properties [8–13]. For instance, a one-pot solvothermal conversion of ortho-phenylenediamine and urea precursors has been reported to result in the synthesis of a number of different CDs that exhibit a wide variety of emission colours, but it was also reported that an extended purification of this mixed product by more than 10 h of column chromatography was required in order to isolate CDs with a specific desired emission colour [8].

Another demonstrated approach for the tailoring of CD properties constitutes the variation of the reaction parameters in the form of the reaction time and temperature [14–16], the reaction solvent during solvothermal conversion [17,18], or the pH during hydrothermal conversion [19]. For instance, Ding and coworkers converted an ortho-phenylenediamine precursor by pyrolysis, and found that an increase of the reaction time from 4 to 10 h and an elevation of the reaction temperature from 180 to 200 °C resulted in a shift of the CD emission colour from blue to red [14].

However, the fact is that a vast majority of the currently employed procedures for obtaining CDs with desired properties either depend on extensive, and therefore costly, purification procedures or time-demanding trial-and-error experimentation. It is therefore motivated to improve the understanding and control of the constituent chemical conversion of the precursor material, so that CDs with desired properties can be synthesized with rational and more efficient methods.

Here, we report on the chemical conversion of an ortho-aminophenol (oAP) precursor, which comprises ortho-disposed amino and hydroxyl groups on a benzene ring. We find that a combination of blue-emitting CDs (bCDs, λ peak = 420 nm) and yellow-emitting CDs (yCDs, λ peak = 565 nm) are simultaneously synthesized by pyrolysis or solvothermal conversion, which subsequently can be separated by extensive purification by column chromatography. A structural characterization reveals that both the bCDs and yCDs feature a flat graphene-like structure and are equipped with hydrophilic hydroxyl and amino surface groups, but are distinguished by that an amino-rich moiety is the fluorophore for the bCDs while a pyridinic-rich moiety is the fluorophore for the yCDs. This implies that the bCDs are formed by a C–N coupling, while the formation of the yCDs constituted the simultaneous formation of CN and C–O bonding. With this information at hand, we could steer the chemical conversion of the oAP precursor towards either solely bCDs or solely yCDs through the introduction of either the Lewis acid AlCl3·6H2O or the Lewis base NaOH as the catalyst in the reactor.

For the catalyst-free pyrolysis, 0.55 g (5 mmol) oAP (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99 %) precursor was first mortared under ambient air for 10 min to decrease the particle size and increase the surface area. The mortared oAP was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. For the catalyst-free solvothermal conversion, 0.22 g (2 mmol) oAP was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol (VWR, absolute, ≥99 %). The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h.

For the catalyst-controlled pyrolysis, 0.55 g (5 mmol) oAP was mixed with 0.08 g (0.33 mmol) AlCl3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99 %), and the mixture was mortared under ambient air for 10 min. The mortared mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. For the catalyst-controlled solvothermal conversion, 0.22 g (2 mmol) oAP was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol, and thereafter 20 μL NaOH (10 M in water) was added to the solution. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h.

The crude product from the above reactions was collected after the reactor had cooled down to room temperature (time ≥12 h). The crude product was purified by column chromatography using silica gel (60 Å, 35–70 μm, Fisher Scientific) as the stationary phase and a mixture of ethyl acetate (Fisher Scientific, HPLC, ≥99.5 %) and petroleum ether (Fisher Scientific, HPLC, 40–60 °C) in a volume ratio of 3:7 as the eluent. The purified CDs were dried by solvent removal through rotary evaporation. We mention that the catalyst-free CDs required two rounds of column chromatography for a good separation of bCDs and yCDs, while the catalyst-controlled crude CDs only required one round of column chromatography for the attainment of high purity. Moreover, since the catalyst-controlled product only comprised one type of fluorescent CD a higher column chromatography pressure could be used, resulting in that the total time for the purification of the catalyst-controlled CDs was only 0.5 h compared to 2 h for the catalyst-free derived CDs.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) samples were prepared by drop-casting a dilute (10 mg L−1) CD-in-ethanol solution on an ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid. The HRTEM images were captured by a cryogenic microscope (Titan Krios, Thermo Fisher Scientific) using an acceleration voltage of 300 kV; the HRTEM was equipped with a direct electron detector (Falcon 4i) and a post-column imaging filter (Selectris) for improved contrast.

The ∼100 μm thick films for the X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were fabricated by drop-casting a concentrated (1 g L−1) CD-in-ethanol solution on a glass substrate or silicon wafer, followed by drying under ambient conditions. The XRD patterns were recorded with an X-ray diffractometer (X'Pert3powder, PANalytical), using a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54056 Å) operating in continuous scan mode. The XRD baseline was estimated and removed with the aid of the HighScore Plus Software. The XPS was collected with an electron spectrometer (Kratos Analytical, AXIS Ultra DLD), using a monochromatic Al Kα source (hν = 1486.6 eV). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on CDs dispersed in KBr (IR grade, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), using a vacuum-bench spectrometer (IFS 66/S, Bruker) operating in diffuse reflectance mode. The FTIR baseline was estimated by a rubber band function (using OPUS 5.0 Software). The Raman spectrum was obtained with a Qontor spectrometer (Renishaw) under excitation at 405 nm using a continuous solid-state laser source. The luminescent background of the Raman spectrum from DBCDs was estimated and removed with the aid of WiRE 2.0 Software.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a two-beam scanning spectrophotometer (Lambda 1050, PerkinElmer). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected with a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh) using a 450 W ozone-free xenon arc lamp as the excitation source. The PL transients were measured with a standard photomultiplier (PMT-900, Edinburgh) as the detector, using a pulsed diode laser (λ exc = 375 nm, pulse width = 60 ps) as the excitation source. The fluorescent lifetime was calculated by fitting the recorded PL transient with the Fluoracle Software. The PLQY was measured with a spectrometer (C9920-02G, Hamamatsu) equipped with an integrating sphere.

Fig. 1 a presents schematically the solid-state pyrolysis (top) and the wet solvothermal conversion (bottom) of a oAP precursor into “crude CDs”. In brief, for the pyrolysis conversion, the as-obtained oAP granule was pretreated by mortaring into a fine powder for an increase of the surface area, and thereafter transferred into an autoclave reactor for a “dry” conversion at 200 °C for 12 h. For the solvothermal conversion, the oAP granule was instead dissolved in ethanol at a concentration of 22 g L−1, and the resulting light-yellow solution subjected to solvothermal conversion in an autoclave reactor at 200 °C for 12 h. More details on the synthesis of the crude CDs can be found in the Experimental Section.

Fig. 1b presents the PL intensity of the crude pyrolysis-converted CDs in ethanol as a function of the excitation wavelength (λ exc) and the PL emission wavelength (λ PL). The PL spectrum of the crude CD solution is found to be strongly dependent on λ exc. Specifically, photoexcitation with shorter excitation wavelengths in the UV region (300 nm < λ exc < 390 nm) yields a blue PL spectrum with a peak wavelength of 420 nm, whereas photoexcitation with blue-green light (400 nm < λ exc < 520 nm) results in a yellow PL spectrum with the peak emission at 565 nm. Fig. S1 shows that the crude solvothermal-converted CDs exhibit essentially identical λ exc-dependent two-color PL emission. The consistent observation of an excitation-wavelength dependent and highly similar two-color PL suggests that both the solid-state pyrolysis and the solvothermal conversion of the oAP precursor has resulted in the formation of two distinct luminophores.

The crude CDs were subsequently purified by repeated column chromatography. A colorless non-luminescent fraction was first collected at the column outlet, followed by a blue luminescent fraction, a yellow luminescent fraction, and finally a dark, non-luminescent fraction. The two luminescent fractions were overlapping in time following the first passage through the column, but a subsequent second passage through the column enabled for clear separation. Importantly, this demonstrated efficient separation of the crude CDs into distinct blue and yellow emissive CDs confirms that the luminescent species indeed are localized on two distinct and separable CD entities. We term the dry blue luminescent fraction for “blue-emitting CDs” (bCDs) and the dry yellow luminescent fraction for “yellow-emitting CDs” (yCDs). Fig. 1c shows photographs of the ethanol solutions of the purified bCDs (left pair of photographs) and the purified yCDs (right pair of photographs) under daylight illumination (left photograph in the pair) and UV excitation (right photograph). We call attention to that the oAP precursor in ethanol solution did not exhibit detectable visible PL (data not shown).

Fig. 1d presents the absorption spectrum of dilute ethanol solutions of the oAP precursor, the bCDs and the yCDs. All three entities feature a similar main absorption band, which is peaking at 285 nm. This common higher-energy absorption band can be assigned to the π-π∗ transition of a smaller aromatic structure in form of a phenyl ring or a larger aromatic graphene structure [20]. The bCDs are distinguished by a broad shoulder band between 320 and 410 nm, while the yCDs instead feature a redshifted broad absorption band between 400 and 530 nm. We tentatively assign these characteristic lower-energy absorption bands to electronic n−π∗ transitions, which are signaling the existence of oxygen and nitrogen heteroatoms, and which is in line with the literature [21–23]. We mention that it was the specific excitation of these characteristic absorption bands that was required in order to obtain the blue and yellow emission of the bCDs and yCDs, respectively, as displayed in the photographs in Fig. 1c.

Fig. 1e and f presents the normalized PL spectra for ethanol solutions of the bCDs and the yCDs, respectively, at different excitation wavelengths, as identified in the inset. The corresponding PLQY values are also presented in the inset. The bCD-in-ethanol solution exhibits a relatively narrow and featureless PL spectrum, with a peak PL wavelength (λ peak) of 420 nm, a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 53 (±3) nm, and a PLQY of 17 (±3) %. The yCD-in-ethanol solution features a redshifted, broadened and slightly more structured PL spectrum, with λ peak = 565 nm, FWHM = 80 (±2) nm and PLQY = 20 (±5) %. The presented “error” represents the variation resulting from the change in the excitation wavelength. Importantly, the observations of an essentially excitation-invariant spectral shape and PLQY are significant in that they imply that a single, but different, luminophore dominates for both the bCDs and the yCDs.

We have further compared the absorption and PL spectra of the two purified CDs with the crude CD solution in Fig. S2. The absorption spectrum of the crude CDs is essentially the average of the absorption spectra of the purified bCDs and yCDs, while the PL spectrum of the bcrude CDs exhibits a similar shape to that of the purified samples at the same excitation wavelength, but with a slightly larger bandwidth.

Fig. 1g and h presents the measured PL transients of dilute ethanol solutions of the bCDs (open blue squares) and the yCDs (solid yellow circles), respectively. The measured data could be fitted with high accuracy with a mono-exponential function (and a constant background), which is indicated by the solid black lines. The derived emissive lifetime was 2.33 ns for the bCDs and 2.76 ns for the yCDs. The finding of a single emissive lifetime is consistent with that each of the CDs contains a single luminophore, while the observation of the short ns-long lifetime implies that both CDs emit by the process of fluorescence.

We now turn our attention to understanding how the oAP precursor can be converted into two distinct fluorophores, and to the deciphering of the chemical structure of the two fluorophore-containing CDs. Fig. 2 a shows the chemical structure of the oAP precursor, which features an amino group and a hydroxyl group attached to a benzene ring in an ortho substitution. The amino and hydroxyl groups are important in that they endow oAP with a number of different chemical reaction pathways [24–26]. Fig. S3 presents four different dimers that can be formed by different chemical couplings of two neighboring oAP molecules [27]. Two of these chemical coupling reactions (Dimers 1 and 2) are particularly interesting since they enable for the formation of larger oligomeric and polymeric structures by a straightforward stepwise addition of oAP molecules (during the release of H atoms).

Fig. 2b presents the C–N coupling reaction between the amino group of one oAP molecule and the carbon atom in the para position of the amino group on a neighboring oAP molecule, which enables the stepwise formation of Dimer 1, Trimer 1, and eventually the “amino-rich moiety 1” that is tentatively incorporated into the bCD in Fig. 2c. Fig. 2d depicts the second stepwise reaction in the form of the simultaneous CN and C–O coupling between the amino group and the phenolic hydroxyl group of one oAP molecule and the two carbon atoms in the para position of the phenolic hydroxyl and amino groups of a neighboring oAP molecule. This simultaneous CN and C–O coupling reaction enables the stepwise formation of Dimer 2, Trimer 2, and the “pyridinic-rich moiety 2” that is incorporated into the yCD structure in Fig. 2e.

A first support for this assignment is provided by time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculations of the absorption and PL spectra of Dimers 1 and 2 and Trimers 1 and 2, as displayed in Fig. 2b–d. The simulated absorption spectra are presented in Fig. S4, while the simulated PL peaks are listed in Table S1. The simulated absorption and PL spectra of Dimer 1 and Trimer 1 are similar to the measured spectra of the bCDs (see Fig. 1d and e), while the simulated data for Dimer 2 and Trimer 2 resemble the measured data for the yCDs (Fig. 1d and f).

An indication of the nature of the surface structure of the CDs is provided by a solubility investigation. We find that both CDs exhibit moderate solubility in hydrophilic solvents, with the bCDs featuring a higher maximum solubility of 1.5 g L−1 in ethanol and 1.0 g L−1 in acetone, compared to the yCDs (1.0 g L−1 in ethanol and 0.6 g L−1 in acetone). We further find that the solubility in hydrophobic solvents is very poor for both CDs, as exemplified by a maximum solubility of <0.1 g L−1 in toluene. We note that the two polymeric structures that result from the two displayed stepwise couplings of the oAP precursor feature the same hydrophilic end groups in the form of a primary amine and a hydroxyl (see Fig. 2b and d). We also note that the non-reacted oAP small-molecule precursor exhibits a much higher solubility of 40 g L−1 in ethanol and acetone (but a relatively low solubility of 2 g L−1 in more hydrophobic toluene). Combined, this suggests (as depicted in Fig. 2c and e) that the surface of both the bCD and the yCDs is covered by a relatively dilute layer of hydrophilic hydroxyls and amines, and that the surface coverage is slightly higher for the bCDs.

Fig. S5 presents the normalized PL spectra and the PLQY of ethanol solutions of bCDs and yCDs as a function of CD concentration. Fig. S5a shows that the major PL peak of the bCDs redshifts marginally from 422 to 425 nm as the bCD concentration increases from 1 to 10 mg L−1, but that a significant shoulder peak increases in magnitude and starts to dominate at 25 mg L−1. Fig. S5b reveals that the entire PL envelope of the yCDs redshifts with increasing concentration, with the main PL peak shifting from 566 to 589 nm. The emission efficiency of both CDs strongly depends on the CD concentration, as exemplified by that the PLQY of the bCDs decreases from 18.6 % to 0.9 %, and that of the yCDs from 20.0 % to 0.7 %, when the CD concentration increases from 1 to 50 mg L−1. We further find that no PL can be observed from neat bCD and yCD powders. The observed redshift of the PL spectrum and the dramatic drop of the PLQY with increasing CD concentration show that both the bCDs and the yCDs suffer from severe aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which has been reported for other CDs as well [28–30].

Fig. S6 presents the normalized PL spectra of the bCDs and the yCDs in a wide range of different solvents, ranging from non-polar ethyl acetate (polarity parameter ET N = 0.228 [31]) to highly polar methanol (ET N = 0.762). We find that both the bCDs and the yCDs exhibit a small positive solvatochromic shift (11 nm for the bCD and 26 nm for the yCD). This minor shift suggests that the interactions between the fluorophores in the CDs and the surrounding solvent are weak, which in turn implies that the fluorophores are embedded within the CD structure where they are protected from the influence of the solvent. Similarly, Fig. S7shows that the PL spectral profile and intensity of the bCDs and yCDs in ethanol solutions are relatively invariant to the presence of five common metal ions in the form of Al3+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+.

Fig. 3 a and b presents HRTEM images of discrete bCDs and yCDs, respectively, deposited on an ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid. The dotted circles indicate the presence of CDs, with the observable ordered feature having a diameter in the range of 2–5 nm. The green histograms in the lower right corner show the measured diameter distributions, and the dashed lines represent the data fitting using a log-normal function. The average observable diameter of the bCDs is determined to be 2.9 ± 0.1 nm, while that of the yCDs is 3.2 ± 0.1 nm. The upper right insets present enlargements of a representative single bCD and a single yCD. We call attention to that the observed interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm in the ordered interior of single CDs of both types is similar to the spacing between the (100) planes of graphene of 0.21 nm [32].

Fig. 3c and d presents the XRD pattern of a 100 μm thick drop-cast film of bCDs and yCDs, respectively, on a glass substrate. A broad diffraction peak is observed at a diffraction angle of 2θ = 24.3° for the bCDs and 2θ = 22.4° for the yCDs. This angle can be translated into an ordered spacing parameter with the aid of Bragg's law, and we find that this XRD-derived spacing is 0.37 nm for the bCD film and 0.40 nm for the yCD film. We note that this spacing distance within the thick CD films are slightly larger than the characteristic 0.34 nm distance between the (002) planes of graphite [33].

We propose that the flat graphene structure, which was observed in discrete CDs by HRTEM (Fig. 3a and b), can stack into a graphitic disk-like disc structure in neat and thick drop-cast CD films, as probed with XRD (Fig. 3c and d). Such a propensity of planar CD sheets to stack into a graphitic-like structure at higher CD concentrations is also in line with the observed strong ACQ of the PL in Fig. S5. The fact that the stacking distance for the neat CD film is slightly larger than that of neat graphite can be rationalized by that non-graphene groups -- e.g., the fluorophores (as implied by Fig. S6) and solubilizing groups -- exist in the bulk and on the surface of the CDs [28,34], and that these non-graphene groups expand the CD lattice compared to that of neat graphite.

Fig. 3e and f presents a XPS survey spectrum of neat films of bCDs and yCDs, respectively. The quantitative analysis, presented in the inset, reveals that both the bCDs and the yCDs are dominated by carbon (C 1s > 85 at.%), but that they also comprise significant contributions from nitrogen (N 1s = 4–5 at.%), and oxygen (O 1s = 6–8 at.%). These observations thus agree with our conclusion above that both the bCDs and the yCDs contain a carbon-only graphene structure, but also that other non-carbon-solely compounds are present in significant quantities.

In order to enable a relative analysis of the graphene and non-graphene fractions, we note that the oAP-derived amino/pyridine-rich structures, depicted in Fig. 2b and d exhibit a carbon atomic concentration of 75 % (excluding hydrogen that is not detected by XPS) whereas the carbon atomic concentration of graphene obviously is 100 %. If we then somewhat naively assume that the CDs solely comprise a combination of the oAP-derived structures and graphene, we can deduce their relative fractions with the following equation:
(1)
75 % ×
(
1 −
G c
)
+ 100 % ×
G c
=
C D
c
where (1-G C) is the fraction of amino/pyridine-rich structure, G C is the fraction of graphene segments in the CDs, and CD C represents the actual atomic carbon concentration in the CDs measured by XPS. This analysis yields that the bCDs comprise 50 % graphene, while the yCDs are slightly graphene-richer at 58 %. Importantly, this analysis suggests that the amino/pyridine-rich structure is a significant part of the CD structure.

Fig. 4 a–f presents high-resolution XPS spectra of a 100 μm thick neat film of (a, c, e) bCDs and (b, d, f) yCDs on a silicon wafer in the (a, b) C 1s, (c, d) N 1s, and (e, f) O 1s regime. The XPS peaks are fitted by a Gaussian-Lorentzian function (70 % Gaussian, 30 % Lorentzian) whereas the background is fitted with a Shirley's function. The sum fitting is represented by the solid black lines, and the observed good agreement with the measured data yields credibility to the fitting procedure.

The analysis of the high-resolution C 1s spectra in Fig. 4a and b gives that the bCDs and yCDs exhibit similar carbon bonding arrangements. More specifically, the carbon atoms in the CDs are primarily (>70 at.%) involved in C–C/CC (284.6 eV) bonding [13], while a second significant fraction of ∼10 at.% of the carbon atoms are part of C–O-(C, H)/C–N structures (285.7 eV), whereas two minor (1–3 at.%) carbon fractions are part of CO (286.6 eV) and O–CO (287.7 eV) bonds. The carbon atoms in the graphene fraction contribute to the majority C–C/CC (284.6 eV) bonding.

The high-resolution N 1s spectra in Fig. 4c and d are deconvoluted into three components in the form of pyridinic nitrogen (399.0 eV), amino nitrogen (400.0 eV) and a minor fraction (<0.5 at.%) of protonated nitrogen (401.3 eV) [35]. Interestingly, the relative distribution of the pyridinic and amino nitrogen atoms (see inset in Fig. 4c for chemical structures) is markedly different for the bCDs and the yCDs, with the amino nitrogen dominating for the bCDs while the pyridinic nitrogen is dominating for the yCDs. In this context, we call attention to the two different chain-like structures displayed in Fig. 4j (and in Fig. 2b and d), and we note with interest that the nitrogen bonding of the bCDs fits the chemical structure of the amino-rich moiety whereas the nitrogen bonding of the yCDs is in good agreement with the pyridinic-rich moiety.

The high-resolution O 1s spectra in Fig. 4e and f are deconvoluted into three peaks, which are assigned to CO (531.2 eV), C–O-(C, H) (532.5 eV) and O∗-CO (533.5 eV) bonding [36]. The only significant difference between the two compounds is that the C–O-(C, H) bonding is twice as prevalent in the bCDs at 4.2 versus 2.0 at.% for the y-CDs. We tentatively attribute this difference to that the surface of the bCDs is richer in oxygen-containing hydroxyl than the yCDs (see Fig. 2c–e), which is in line with the observed higher solubility of the bCDs in hydrophilic solvents.

Fig. 4g–i presents the normalized FTIR spectra of the oAP precursor (as a reference), the bCDs, and the yCDs dispersed in a KBr powder. The FTIR spectra of the two CDs exhibit broadened bands compared to the oAP precursor, which is in line with that oAP-based oligomers are dispersed into a carbonized structure (see Fig. 2c and e). Key vibrational modes are identified by the dashed grey rectangles. The vibrational band between 3550 and 3050 cm−1 is assigned to phenolic -OH and amino -NH2 symmetric stretching vibrations [8]. This band is stronger for the bCDs than the yCDs, which agrees with that both the amine and the hydroxyl are part of the repeat unit of the amino-rich moiety, but absent from the repeat unit of the pyridinic-rich moiety. The fact that this band is detectable also in the yCDs can be explained by that amines and hydroxyls exist as surface groups (although at low density) and by that the pyridinic-rich moiety is prevalent in the form of short-chain oligomers with a concomitant significant portion of amine and hydroxyl end groups (see Fig. 4j).

The band between 3050 and 2850 cm−1 is attributed to C–H asymmetric stretching vibrations of aromatic rings [37], while the band between 1550 and 1440 cm−1 is due to CC–C symmetric vibrations [20]. Both bands are originating in the graphene component of the CDs. We note that the strongest ν(CC–C) peak has shifted from 1495 cm−1 in bCDs to 1460 cm−1 in yCDs, which can be a manifestation of that the graphene environment is different. The ν(C–OH) band between 1410 and 1320 cm−1 is strongest for the bCDs, which is in line with it comprising the amino-rich moiety. In contrast, the ν(C–O–C) band at 1100 cm−1 is strongest for the yCDs [38], which is in agreement with it comprising the pyridinic-rich moiety. Finally, the N–H wagging band at 750 cm−1 is, as expected, strongest for the amino-containing bCDs [39].

Fig. S8 presents the Raman spectra of 100 μm thick neat films of bCDs and yCDs deposited on a silicon wafer. The “graphitic” G band is located between 1530 and 1620 cm−1, while the “disordered” D band is positioned between 1300 and 1420 cm−1 [40,41]. The I G/I D intensity ratio is high at 0.83 and 0.94 for the bCDs and yCDs, respectively, which confirms that both carbon dots comprise a significant graphitic component and also that the degree of graphitization a slightly higher for the yCDs.

To summarize, the collected structural data imply that the bCDs and yCDs feature the structures depicted in Fig. 2c and e. Both the bCDs and the yCDs comprise a graphene-like interior with a diameter of ∼3 nm, and a low concentration of solubilizing hydrophilic hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface. They are however significantly differentiated by the presence of an amino-rich moiety in the bCD, and a pyridinic-rich moiety in the yCD. These distinguishing amino- or pyridinic-rich moieties function as the fluorophores, which are embedded within the graphene-like interior of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlling the conversion reaction for a desired emission colour
ce:section.ce:para: With this structural information at hand, we attempted to design the chemical conversion process so that it results in a selective and efficient synthesis of only one of the CDs. We specifically identified two different catalysts in the form of the Lewis acid, AlCl3·6H2O, and the Lewis base, NaOH, which we anticipated should direct the conversion of the oAP precursor towards solely bCDs or yCDs, respectively.

Fig. 5 a presents our anticipated selective chemical conversion of the oAP precursor towards solely bCDs through the action of the AlCl3·6H2O catalyst. The reaction can start with the Al3+ cation coordinating the lone electron pair of the -NH2 group of oAP, followed by the release of H+ and the formation of a covalent (Al2+-O-Ph) bond (1). The electron-accepting capacity of (1) results in that its carbon atom at the para position (opposite to the amino group) is attacked by the amino group of a neighboring oAP, leading to the formation of (2) during the release of H−. We mention that the Al ions creates an acidic environment in the reactor, which restricts the reactivity of the Ph-OH group. (2) is thereafter converted to (3) through the release of H+. Finally, a proton replaces the coordinated Al3+ ion for the conversion of (3) to (4), which is the amino-rich Dimer 1 depicted in Fig. 2b.

Fig. 5b presents the reaction conditions at which the pyrolysis conversion of oAP with the aid of the AlCl3·6H2O catalyst was practically executed. We call attention to that the reaction conditions are identical to those presented in Fig. 1a. However, it is notable that, while the pyrolysis conversion depicted in the top part of Fig. 1a resulted in two-color PL from a combination of bCDs and yCDs (see Fig. 1b), the addition of the AlCl3·6H2O catalyst resulted in the desired formation of solely bCD, as verified by the blue PL spectrum displayed in Fig. 5c.

Fig. 5d discloses how we anticipate that the chemical conversion of the oAP precursor can be directed towards the selective formation of yCDs with the aid of a NaOH catalyst. The OH− (from the NaOH catalyst) reacts with the phenolic group (-OH) of oAP resulting in the release of H2O and the formation of (5). A neighboring oAP forms a hydrogen bond with ethanol to form (6). The lone electron pair on the O atom of the phenolic group (Ph-O-) of (5) then attacks the carbon atom at the para position (opposite to the amino group) of (6) by releasing a H−, leading to the formation of (7). A cyclization reaction resulting in the formation of (8) is initiated by releasing three H radicals, due to the high reactivity of the -NH2 of (7). Finally, ethanol is released from (8) for the synthesis of the pyridinic-rich structure (9), which is identical to Dimer 2 in Fig. 2d.

Fig. 5e presents the reaction conditions during which we solvothermally converted oAP in the presence of NaOH. The reaction conditions were again selected to be identical to the corresponding catalyst-free conversion displayed in the lower panel of Fig. 1a. Importantly, and with satisfaction, we find that the addition of the NaOH catalyst resulted in the desired selective synthesis of yCDs, as demonstrated by the recording of their characteristic PL spectrum in Fig. 5f.

We finally wish to mention that the introduction of an appropriate catalyst not only steered the conversion reaction of the precursor in a desired direction, but also improved the CD product yield and streamlined the purification process. The non-catalyst conversion of the oAP precursor required two rounds of column chromatography purification for the collection of bCDs with a product yield of ∼3 % and yCDs with a product yield of ∼2 %. The introduction of the catalyst resulted in that only one round of column chromatography purification was required in order to obtain a high-quality CD product, and the product yield was improved to ∼15 % for the bCDs and ∼8 % for the yCDs. Furthermore, we find that the catalyst-controlled-synthesis does not result in catalyst contamination of the bCDs and yCDs, as the XPS survey spectra in Fig. S9 shows that they both are free from contamination by Al and Na.

We report on the solid-state pyrolysis or solvothermal conversion of an oAP precursor compound into a combination of blue-emitting bCDs and yellow-emitting yCDs, which can be separated by repeated column chromatography. Through systematic characterization, we establish that both CDs exhibit a flat graphene-like structure and hydrophilic hydroxyl and amino surface groups, but that they are distinguished by that the bCDs contain an amino-rich moiety as the fluorophore while the yCDs instead comprise a pyridinic-rich moiety for the fluorophore. This shows that the bCDs are formed by an activation of the amino group of the oAP precursor, whereas the formation of the yCDs depended upon the simultaneous activation of both the amino and hydroxyl groups of oAP. With this information available, we attempted to direct the conversion reaction of the precursor for the attainment of one specific structure and one emission colour through the addition of an appropriate catalyst. Specifically, we showed that by introducing the Lewis acid AlCl3·6H2O into the reaction vessel, we could selectively synthesize bCDs from the oAP precursor, while the addition of the Lewis base NaOH resulted in the selective synthesis of yCDs. We also found that the addition of the catalyst simplified the purification process and resulted in an improved product yield. To summarize, we show that the synthesis of CDs from a single precursor can be directed towards different and distinct chemical structures and emission colours through the addition of a tuned catalyst, which is relevant since it represents a tuned CD synthesis with rational and efficient means instead of time-consuming trial-and-error experimentation.

Junkai Ren: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kaiqi Ye: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Henry Opoku: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhiqiang Li: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ludvig Edman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jia Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Dr. Cheng Choo Lee for valuable advice during the TEM measurements, and the Umeå Core Facility for Electron Microscopy (UCEM), the Vibrational Spectroscopy Core Facility (ViSp), and the XPS platform at Umeå University for technical support. The authors also thank Dr. Yincai Xu at Linköping University for valuable input regarding reaction mechanisms, and Prof. Christian Reichardt at Philipps University Marburg for appreciated input on solvatochromism. The authors finally acknowledge generous support from J. C. Kempes Minnes Stipendiefond (SMK-21-0015 and SMK-1956), the Swedish Research Council (2019–02345, 2020–04437 and 2021–04778), Bertil & Britt Svenssons stiftelse för belysningsteknik (2021 höst-14 and 2022 höst-31), the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation for a Proof of Concept grant (KAW 2022.0381), the Wallenberg Initiative Materials Science for Sustainability (WISE) funded by the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation (WISE-AP01-D02), and the European Union through an ERC Advanced Grant (ERC, InnovaLEC, 101096650).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

PL spectrum of solvothermal-converted crude CDs; chemical structure of the four dimers of oAP precursor; PL spectra of bCDs and yCDs as a function of concentration and solvents; PL spectra of bCDs and yCDs in the presence of metal ions; Raman spectra of bCDs and yCDs; simulated properties of the oAP Dimers and Trimers.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2024.119706.

Wang, Carbon nanodots: A metal-free, easy-to-synthesize, and benign emitter for light-emitting electrochemical cells, Nano Res., 15 (2022) 5610-5618. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-022-4126-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-quenching origin of carbon dots and the guideline for their solid-state luminescence
